description:
Your role is a release-engineer / capacity-planner who must prepare the next
    minor release (v1.5.0) of the “capacity-planner” project located at
    
        /home/user/capacity-planner
    
    The repository already exists and is initialised with Git.  
    It currently reports version “1.4.3”.

    Goals (all must be completed):

    1.  Create a *Git branch* dedicated to this release.  
        Branch name MUST be exactly

            release/v1.5.0

    2.  Semantic-version bump  
        •   Edit the plain-text file  

                /home/user/capacity-planner/VERSION  

            so that it **contains only the single line**  

                1.5.0  

            followed by a trailing newline.  
        •   Do not leave any leading/trailing spaces or additional
            lines.

    3.  CHANGELOG update  
        •   Pre-pend a new top-level section to  

                /home/user/capacity-planner/CHANGELOG.md  

            that follows the exact layout below (including the blank line
            between header and sub-headers, the order of sub-headers, and the
            markdown bullet character “-”).  Replace no other content.
        
                ## [1.5.0] - 2023-10-05

                ### Added
                - Feature: Predictive capacity planning for next quarter (#42)

                ### Changed
                - Updated dependency matrix

                ### Performance
                - CPU usage now 15% lower on average; memory footprint reduced by 10 MiB.

    4.  Capacity report  
        •   Inside the repository, create the directory  

                /home/user/capacity-planner/reports  

            if it does not already exist.  
        •   Read **all** “*.csv” files stored in  

                /home/user/capacity-planner/logs/  

            and for each file calculate  
              •   the maximum value found in column “cpu_percent”  
              •   the maximum value found in column “mem_mb”  
        •   Produce a summary file named  

                /home/user/capacity-planner/reports/capacity_report_1.5.0.csv  

            with the following exact structure (comma-separated, UNIX line-endings):

                file,peak_cpu,peak_memory
                <filename1>,<max_cpu1>,<max_mem1>
                <filename2>,<max_cpu2>,<max_mem2>
                …

            The header line must be exactly as written above.
            Lines may appear in any order, but every “*.csv” file in
            the logs directory must be represented.

    5.  Commit & tag  
        •   Stage **all** modified / new files.  
        •   Create a single commit whose commit-message **header line** is

                chore(release): 1.5.0

            (The rest of the commit message body may be left empty.)  
        •   Add an *annotated* Git tag named “v1.5.0” that points to that commit
            and has the tag message “1.5.0”.

    6.  Verification artefact  
        •   To allow automated grading, also write a plain-text log file

                /home/user/capacity-planner/reports/verification.log

            containing exactly three lines (no extras, no indentation):

                BRANCH=release/v1.5.0
                VERSION=1.5.0
                TAG=v1.5.0

    Constraints & hints:

    •   You have normal user privileges only.  
    •   The Git repository already exists; do not re-initialise it.  
    •   Do not delete or rename existing files.  
    •   Use only POSIX-compliant shell tools already available in a minimal
        Ubuntu-based container (awk, sed, sort, git, etc.).  
    •   Ensure that every file you edit ends with a UNIX newline (LF).  
    •   Follow the name/path conventions **exactly**—the grader is strict.
truth:
Initial repository layout before the agent starts
    -----------------------------------------------
    /home/user/capacity-planner
    ├── VERSION                 (contents: "1.4.3\n")
    ├── CHANGELOG.md            (contains earlier release notes; agent will prepend)
    ├── logs
    │   ├── web.csv             (see content below)
    │   └── db.csv              (see content below)
    └── (standard .git directory with an initial commit)

    Contents of /home/user/capacity-planner/logs/web.csv
    ----------------------------------------------------
    timestamp,cpu_percent,mem_mb
    00:00,12,100
    00:01,20,110
    00:02,18,95
    (file ends with newline)

    Contents of /home/user/capacity-planner/logs/db.csv
    ---------------------------------------------------
    timestamp,cpu_percent,mem_mb
    00:00,30,210
    00:01,45,220
    00:02,40,215
    (file ends with newline)

    Expected artefacts after successful completion
    ----------------------------------------------
    1.  /home/user/capacity-planner/VERSION  
        → "1.5.0\n" exactly.

    2.  /home/user/capacity-planner/CHANGELOG.md  
        → New section inserted exactly as shown in the public spec,
          appearing before older sections.

    3.  /home/user/capacity-planner/reports/capacity_report_1.5.0.csv  
        → Exact lines (order may vary, but header must be first):
            file,peak_cpu,peak_memory
            web.csv,20,110
            db.csv,45,220
        → File ends with newline.

    4.  /home/user/capacity-planner/reports/verification.log  
        → Lines exactly:
            BRANCH=release/v1.5.0
            VERSION=1.5.0
            TAG=v1.5.0
        → File ends with newline.

    5.  Git state  
        •   Current branch: release/v1.5.0  
        •   One new commit on that branch whose header line is
            “chore(release): 1.5.0”.
        •   Tag “v1.5.0” (annotated) pointing at that commit.
        •   Working tree clean.

    Automated tests will:
    •   Verify the content of VERSION, CHANGELOG.md, capacity_report_1.5.0.csv
        and verification.log exactly.
    •   Check that every log file listed under /logs is represented in the report.
    •   Inspect Git for the correct branch, commit message, and tag.

    The agent must therefore satisfy all file-content and Git-state conditions
    for the task to be marked as successful.
